topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, searing against my retinas. I stood frozen at the edge of the room, my breath catching in my throat, watching the canvases I had poured my soul into—my life distilled onto linen—being appraised by a crowd that didn't know me. A woman with a sharp, assessing gaze circled my latest piece, her head tilted, a small, patronizing smile playing on her lips as she gestured toward the vibrant blues and deep shadows. My hands clenched into tight fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white. Every muscle in my body tightened, a sudden, cold dread pooling in my stomach. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the echoing hallway, but my feet were rooted to the polished floor. The silence in my head was deafening, a hollow space where my creative voice used to reside.

[story 2]

"This texture is fascinating," a voice drawled from across the room. I couldn't move. My vision tunneled, focusing only on the way the harsh spotlights caught the impasto on my sculpture. It wasn't just the art; it was the invasion. These were my private confessions, my unguarded moments made visible, now being dissected and consumed by strangers. A tremor ran through my hands, making the small sketchbook I clutched feel flimsy and useless. I took a sharp, shallow breath, tasting dust and expensive perfume. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my neck. I wanted to scream, to demand that these objects be returned to the sanctuary of my studio, but the sound wouldn't come out. My eyes darted around, searching desperately for an exit, for any shadow deep enough to hide the sheer exposure of it all.

[story 3]

I watched from the periphery as someone pointed, their laughter bright and careless, directed squarely at my portrait. The sound seemed amplified, bouncing off the white walls and settling deep in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. A wave of nausea washed over me, a sickening lurch in my core. My skin prickled, as if exposed to a sudden, biting cold despite the warmth of the room. I took a step back, bumping against a nearby pedestal, and the sudden movement made the surrounding figures turn their attention toward me, their curiosity replacing their critique. My mouth went dry. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of sheer distress that made me want to dissolve. *They don't understand,* I thought, the realization sharp and painful. *They have no right to see this.*
